content.text:
Wrap the demo gifs in @README.md in img tags. Example


(Install Missing Tools)

# Session Bridge

> This is an **unofficial** VS Code extension. It is not developed by, endorsed by, or in any way related to the [Entire](https://entire.io) team.

Session Bridge is an unofficial VS Code extension for browsing [Entire](https://entire.io) checkpoints and the committed sessions captured in those checkpoints from the current repository.

## Requirements
- Visual Studio Code `1.10` or newer (or editors compatible with VS Code `1.10+` APIs)
- [Entire CLI](github.com/entireio/cli) version `0.5.5` or newer

## Using the extension
Session Bridge works with any git repository that has [Entire](https://docs.entire.io/introduction) configured to capture your AI agent sessions. You don't need to be logged in to your [Entire](https://entire.io) account to view your sessions. 

Open any Entire managed git repository and the `Session Bridge (Entire)` status bar will show at the bottom. 

Select or open the installed Session Bridge extension to view your checkpoints.

## Features

- **Browse your committed checkpoints.**
  ![Browse Checkpoints](https://github.com/savekirk/session-bridge/blob/main/docs/assets/checkpoints.gif)

- **View diffs of changes within a checkpoint.**
  ![View Diffs](https://github.com/savekirk/session-bridge/blob/main/docs/assets/view-diffs.gif)

- **View sessions within a selected checkpoint.**
  ![List Sessions](https://github.com/savekirk/session-bridge/blob/main/docs/assets/sessions.gif)

- **View details of a session.**
  ![Session Details](https://github.com/savekirk/session-bridge/blob/main/docs/assets/session-details.gif)

- **View status of active sessions.**
  ![Active Sessions Status](https://github.com/savekirk/session-bridge/blob/main/docs/assets/active-sessions.gif)

- **Run [Entire CLI](github.com/entireio/cli) commands**

## Known Issues

 - Some of the transcript might not be properly parsed.

## Release Notes

### 0.0.1

Initial release

## Contributing

Contributions are welcome! Please see our [Contribution Guide](CONTRIBUTING.md) for details on how to get started, coding standards, and our development workflow.

thoughts.description: I've begun parsing the `README.md` file to pinpoint the existing GIF image lines. My next step involves constructing the HTML `img` tags. I'll need to extract the source URLs and then replace the markdown image syntax with the requested structure, ensuring proper formatting.

thoughts.subject: Analyzing Image Paths
thoughts.description: I'm currently focused on the image paths. The existing `README.md` uses GitHub blob URLs, while the example employs a local path structure. I need to determine the optimal approach: either replace with relative paths or retain the existing URLs. I'll need to consider how the user expects the file to be viewed, and whether the URLs should be kept.

thoughts.description: Now I'm ready to replace the markdown image syntax. I'm focusing on the first example. I'll translate `![Browse Checkpoints](https://github.com/savekirk/session-bridge/blob/main/docs/assets/checkpoints.gif)` to the appropriate HTML. The caption will be derived from the surrounding text in the README.md file, which I'll have to parse. The final HTML should match the user's template, with the correct image path and width.

thoughts.subject: Parsing and Structuring HTML
thoughts.description: I'm now extracting the relevant context surrounding each GIF in the markdown. This involves using the existing alt text for the `<em>` tag and generating the HTML structure to match the user's specification. I am using the `docs/assets/` path for the `src` attribute, as well. I'm focusing on the first GIF to test the output.

thoughts.subject: Converting Image Paths
thoughts.description: I've decided to proceed with replacing the markdown image syntax, first, by parsing the `README.md` to identify the GIF lines. I will then replace them with the specified HTML structure. I'll use the alt text as the caption. I am using relative paths within the `docs/assets/` directory.

messages.content: I will replace the markdown images with the HTML structure, using relative paths and indentation to maintain the list structure.

thoughts.subject: Refactoring Image Handling
thoughts.description: I'm currently working to replace markdown images with their HTML counterparts. The aim is to use relative paths for the images, specifically following `docs/assets/...`, and incorporate the alt text as captions using `<em>` tags. This shift should allow more granular control over the image's presentation, and allow the images to be better handled by the site.

thoughts.subject: Converting Markdown to HTML
thoughts.description:
I've just implemented the conversion from markdown image syntax to HTML `` tags, placed beneath each image. I've also implemented the inclusion of `

` tags to center the images.

thoughts.subject: Considering Indentation Now
thoughts.description: I'm now contemplating how to handle indentation in the conversion process. The existing markdown uses two spaces before the image syntax, so I'll preserve that. It appears from examples that no further indentation within the HTML tags is needed, so I'll keep the formatting flat for simplicity.

thoughts.subject: Applying List Item Formatting
thoughts.description: I'm now focusing on formatting the HTML within the list items, using the example as a guide. Given that the markdown examples start with two spaces, I've decided to indent the generated HTML to ensure it appears correctly within the list structure. I'll also ensure consistent use of `width=75%` instead of `width="75%"`. Finally, I'll execute the changes using a single replacement operation.
